back_translation: 'Why does the Almighty not appoint times for judgment? Why do those who know Him never see His day of judgment?
People move boundary stones; they steal flocks and pasture them.
They drive away the donkey of the fatherless and take the widow's ox as a pledge.
They push the needy off the road and force all the poor of the land into hiding.
Behold, like wild donkeys in the desert, the poor must go out to forage for food; the wasteland provides food for their children.
They gather fodder in the fields and glean the leftover grapes in the vineyards of the wicked.
They spend the night naked, without clothing, with no covering against the cold.
They are drenched by mountain rains and huddle against the rock for lack of shelter.
The fatherless infant is snatched from the breast, and the child of the poor is seized as a pledge for debt.
The poor go about naked, without clothing; they carry the sheaves yet still go hungry.
They press out olive oil within the walls of their masters and tread the winepresses, yet go thirsty.
From the city people groan, and the souls of the wounded cry out for help, yet God charges no one with wrong.
There are also those who rebel against the light, not knowing its ways or staying on its paths.
When the sunlight is gone, the murderer rises to kill the poor and needy, and in the night he is like a thief.
The eye of the adulterer watches for twilight, thinking, 'No eye will see me,' and he covers his face.
In the dark they break into houses; by day they shut themselves in, refusing to acknowledge the light.
For to them the dawn is like deep darkness; they are familiar with the terrors of thick darkness.
They are like foam on the surface of the water; their portion of the land is cursed, so no one turns toward their vineyards.
As drought and heat consume the melting snow, so Sheol carries off those who have sinned.
The womb that bore them forgets them; the worm feeds on them with delight; they are remembered no more, and injustice is broken like a tree.
They take advantage of the barren woman who has no child and show no kindness to the widow.
But God drags away the mighty by His power; though they rise up, they have no assurance of life.
He gives them a sense of security and support, but His eyes are fixed on their ways.
They are exalted for a moment, then they are gone; they are brought low and gathered up like everyone else, and cut off like heads of grain.
If this is not so, who can prove me a liar and reduce my words to nothing?'
